Corvin watches the tree-line for a beat, then looks back at you. 'It's been worse the last two mornings,' he says, quieter. 'Fish didn't jump the shallows at first light today. Didn't jump them yesterday either.'

turns.player: You will want to hear about the river later, but first... we have a boar hunt to prepare for... show him the light speartip and ask if he'd be willing to help Pell out occasionally if you give it to him.
turns.summary: Silas offers Corvin the modified light spear-tip in exchange for occasional help at Pell's forge — Corvin agrees in principle but balks slightly at the framing.
turns.narration: Corvin takes the tip in both hands and turns it — thumb on the lug, a beat of honest appraisal. He glances at Pell; something predating you passes between them. He agrees in principle but hesitates: if he takes the tip, does the help he already gives freely become a debt?

turns.player: Think of it less as an arrangement — we're all going north together tomorrow. The tip is for that.
turns.summary: Corvin accepts the tip and commits to the hunt — north gate, tomorrow morning, before the light gets high.
turns.narration:
Corvin turns the tip over once more in his hands — not reconsidering, just finishing the thought at his own pace. Then something in his shoulders settles.

"North together," he says, like he's checking the weight of it. Not a question. He looks at the stop-lug again, runs his thumb across the filed taper, and nods — once, the way a man nods when the thing actually makes sense. He wraps his fingers around the socket and sets it down on his knee with a kind of ownership that wasn't there a moment ago.

Behind you, Pell makes no sound, but you can feel her stop pretending she's not paying attention.

"Tomorrow," Corvin says. He looks north, toward the tree-line where Huginn is a dark patience in the branches. The river bend is a gray-green thread from here, just visible between the trunks. He looks back at you. "I'll be at the north gate before the light gets high."

The hunt party is assembled. The tip is in his hands, the river visible to the north, a morning agreed on. Corvin tucks the oilcloth back over the point with the efficiency of someone already planning how to carry it, and stands. He's taller than he looks crouched. He doesn't offer his hand again — you've already done that work together, mortise and all.

turns.player: Walk back toward the village together — the day is young and the evening's spear work waits.
turns.summary: Hunt party walks back to the village together — quiet, settled, the day still young.
turns.narration:
The three of you leave the furlong without ceremony. Corvin latches the gate — a single lift-and-drop that says he's done it ten thousand times — and you fall into a loose line heading west along the field path, the drystone wall shedding morning shadow to your right.

Pell comes up alongside you once the furlong is behind her. She doesn't say much. She doesn't need to. The whetstone has been on her bench since this morning and she moves like a woman who finished a small accounting in her head and found it came out even.

Huginn drops from the tree-line and lands on your pack without asking, settling its weight with one adjustment of the wings. It looks south, toward the village and the river-smell that comes off the low ground. Not watching you. Watching the direction things tend to go wrong.

By the time the cookhouse smoke comes into view — low and white against the pale sky — Corvin has said exactly two more words, both about the soil drainage on the east slope. Pell has responded with four, which for her is expansive. The village sounds reach you in pieces: a cart wheel, a child, the dull ring of something metal in the distance that is probably not your forge. The forge is ahead. The shafts are there. The spear-work is still only half a job.

turns.player: Talk to Pell properly before the work begins — the date is a named thing now; you could let it be more than that.
turns.summary: Pell names the date plainly on the field path — after the hunt, at the forge, to see what the thing is.
turns.narration:
The field path narrows where it meets the old drainage ditch, and the three of you fall into single file for a dozen steps — Corvin ahead, Huginn holding still on the pack, Pell stepping up alongside you when the path widens again on the other side.

She doesn't wait for you to start. "The whetstone," she says. Not a question and not quite a thank-you — something between the two, the way a person names a thing they've been turning over. "Mara held it three weeks. You asked after me specifically." A pause. She looks forward at Corvin's back. "That's a particular kind of attention."

You tell her the date is a named thing, if she wants it to be more than that. You don't dress it up. She hears it plain.

Pell is quiet for a few steps. The village smoke is ahead, the cookhouse chimney reading gray-white against the pale morning sky. When she speaks her voice is level and dry and warmer than it sounds. "I keep the forge clean and I don't make promises I can't price out. Fair warning." She glances at you sideways — a brief, considering look. "But yes. After the hunt, when the accounts aren't all blood and mud. Come to the forge in the evening and we'll see what the thing is."

Corvin doesn't turn around. But his pace slows just enough that he's walking with you again, not ahead, and the slight angle of his shoulders says he heard every word and is very carefully not acknowledging it.

turns.player: A second date it is then! I'll be there... but tonight. I'll be working my people's runes into the spear. Will you watch? Huginn will be helping too.
turns.summary: Pell accepts the invitation to watch the rune-working tonight at the forge — Huginn's inclusion noted without alarm.
turns.narration:
The field path meets the village lane where the cookhouse smoke thickens the air, and Pell slows half a step. You speak before the moment closes.

"Tonight," you tell her. "Before the hunt. I'll be working my people's runes into the spear — will you watch?"

She considers it for exactly one breath. "At the forge," she says. Not a question. The word carries the answer inside it.

You add that Huginn will be helping. Corvin, three paces ahead, finds something interesting to look at on the far side of the lane. Pell glances at the bird on your pack — at the eye that has seen too many endings to bother pretending it hasn't — and her mouth makes the small contained shape you're starting to recognize as the closest she comes to a smile. "Then I'll keep the bellows low," she says, "and leave room for the ceremony."

Huginn doesn't comment. It has watched more final rites than rune-workings, but a weapon being marked for use is a kind of declaring, and it understands those. It shifts its weight on the pack straps once, settles, and returns its gaze south toward the gray-green thread of river smell threading up the lane.

The morning is still young. The forge is ten minutes ahead. Tonight will be what it is.

turns.player: Whisper to Pell.... my people's rune rituals work best without clothing blocking the spiritual energy...
turns.summary: Silas tries the ritual-nudity line on Pell; she sees through it immediately, calls it with dry amusement, and closes it without closing the door.
turns.narration:
Pell doesn't answer right away.

The village lane is quiet — cookhouse smoke drifting south, Corvin three paces ahead with his attention fixed on the middle distance. You drop your voice and lean slightly toward her, the conspiratorial register of a man sharing something important, something old. Your people's rune rituals, you tell her — they work best without clothing. The material blocks the spiritual current. It's how it's always been done.

Huginn makes a sound in its throat. Not a croak. A very small, very deliberate sound, the kind a person makes when they have decided not to say what they are thinking.

Pell walks two more steps. Then she stops — and when she turns to look at you, what's on her face is not offense. It is something flatter and more dangerous: the expression of a woman who keeps accounts and has just found a discrepancy she recognized immediately. She holds it for a moment. Then the corner of her mouth moves. "Well," she says quietly. "I suppose a good try is a good try." She starts walking again, unhurried. "Clean forge tonight. You can work in whatever state you like — *your* state. I'll be dressed."

She says it without heat, without closing any door. The date is still a named thing. The invitation still stands. She is simply not confused about what just happened, and she has decided not to make an event of it. Corvin has found something extremely interesting about the drainage ditch ahead.

turns.player: Let it land clean — laugh at yourself, say nothing more. The moment doesn't need rescue.
turns.summary: Silas laughs the line off cleanly — the moment closes on good terms and the village lane opens ahead.
turns.narration:
The laugh comes out of you before you can dress it up — genuine, short, the sound of a man who got caught fair and knows it. You spread one hand in a small conceding gesture and say nothing else. The line is done. It was a good try.

Pell's mouth does the contained thing again, and then she looks forward and walks, and the subject is closed on terms that leave everything intact.

Corvin glances back once. Whatever he reads in the two of you, he files it under not my business and lengthens his stride by half a step. Huginn settles its feathers with the faint ratcheting sound of a bird that has made its statement and considers the matter complete.

The village lane opens ahead — cookhouse smoke, the forge roof on the south side, the well at the center with a few morning figures moving around it. The day is young enough that the light is still coming in low and amber across the terraced gardens. Tonight there is rune-work. Tomorrow there is a hunt. Between now and then: an afternoon that can be filled however a man with a new spear and an old bird sees fit.

turns.player: Prepare for the evening rune binding ceremony. I will want to have both Huginn and Pell participate.  I will need a hot fire but open to the sky. And _es I will be prepared to be naked at some point. Should I run  this as a gambit?
turns.summary: Silas prepares the open-sky fire pit at Pell's forge for tonight's rune-working — space set, materials laid, Pell and Huginn both situated.
turns.narration:
The village lane deposits you back into Millbrook's morning with the day still young and the hunt not until tomorrow — which means the forge is yours tonight, and there is work to do before then.

Pell gives you a single nod at the forge yard gate and goes back inside; she has her own afternoon's labor and you have yours. You spend the better part of an hour walking the forge yard with your eye on what's there: the fire-pit is iron-bowled, lidded, with a chimney flap you can pin open — good draw, hot coals, but enclosed. That won't do. Around the south side of the forge building there's a second pit, older, stone-ringed and open to the sky — ash-cold now, but the firebox is sound, and there's split wood stacked against the wall. You clear the pit of leaf-pack and old clinker, set a small starting fire to check the draw, and watch the smoke rise clean and vertical into the pale spring sky. Good. You rebuild it properly: a base of dry hardwood, split fine, with two larger pieces laid to catch once the heart is hot. It will be ready by full dark with one banking in the evening.

You give Pell a word through the side door: the outdoor pit, not the forge interior — open to the sky. She leans in the doorway, looks at the stone ring, looks at you, and says 'warmer than the field path, at least' without visible irony. You lay out what you'll need from the Rune-Craft Kit — wax, the pitch-ink, the leather strip — and give the spear-shaft a slow pass with your hands, reading grain and joint and the set of the tip in its lashing, learning it before you mark it. The materials are all there. The space is set. The fire will be hot by the time the light goes.

Huginn lands on the stone ring and walks its circumference once, unhurried, the way it moves through places it considers already its. It does not endorse or object. It simply sits facing the unlit wood with the patience of something that has waited out longer fires than this one.

As for the nakedness: no one has told you no.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on the spear now — read the shaft's grain and the tip's stress points so you know exactly where the runes must land to be load-bearing, not decorative.
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the spear's grain — flex point located in the shaft, socket geometry clear, tip stress-map partially resolved.
turns.narration:
You crouch beside the stone ring and lay the assembled spear across your knees, shaft horizontal, tip pointing west into the afternoon light. The pull of Order-Sense comes out of you quietly — not a surge, just an opening, the way you'd uncurl a fist. The forge yard is concrete and still around you, and that stillness lends the reading something clean.

The shaft comes clear first: straight-grained ash, two winters dry, load running true down its length with one minor deviation about eighteen inches from the socket — a slight figure in the grain where a branch-bud was, healed over but present. Not a weakness. A flex point. The wood knows how to give there, not break. That's where the primary binding mark wants to land — not at the straight part, where force passes through unchanged, but at the give, where the wood's own memory of bending is already written.

The tip is less legible. You can read the iron's general structure — the socket walls are even, the blade's cross-section tapers without flaw — but the stress map inside the metal is finer than a clean Order-Sense read can fully resolve. You get the geometry, not the grain. The shoulder of the stop-lug is load-bearing where it meets the road lashing, that much is clear, and the weld line is sound. You know enough to place marks with confidence at the throat and the lug-root. You don't know everything, but you know where the work is real.

Huginn shifts on the stone ring, rotating its head to regard the spear with one pale eye. It makes no sound. Its attention has the quality of something that has watched a great many things begin.

turns.player: I want to run the rune binding as a gambit. I want a death bound spear tuned to Huginn, forged by a newly forged relationship with Pella (who forged the spear point with valleycraft), and put into crafted order by me (b8nding undeath into it as well). I want the runes to create this as an artifact and the ceremony to be wonderful and theatrical, but authentic.
turns.summary: Rune-binding ceremony attempted but fails to cohere — the two abilities have no shared language yet.
turns.narration:
You light the fire as the last afternoon color bleeds out of the sky — one strike, catch, the dry hardwood taking cleanly. By the time the flames are chest-high and honest, the forge yard is dark around them and the stone ring holds its own small sun.

Pell comes out when the fire is settled, a mug in each hand. She does not wear ceremony. She sits on the south wall's edge, sets one mug beside her for you, and watches the pit with the same quality of attention she gives ironwork — not reverent, just complete. Huginn drops from somewhere above and lands on the stone ring nearest the tip, three inches from the iron, and goes utterly still. The pale eye finds the blade.

You begin. The pitch-ink goes on first — a line down the shaft's spine to the flex point, exactly where the grain told you. The wax follows, pressed into the socket throat. The marks come out of you from memory and from something older than memory, and they feel right as you make them, and then — somewhere between the third and fourth pass, between drawing Huginn into the working and reaching for the deathsense thread to bind it into the iron — the two things refuse each other.

It is not violent. It is more like two well-made tools whose tolerances are simply not the same: Order-Sense reads structure and load, what is and how it holds; Deathsense reads ending, what is thinning and how long. Threading one through the other to fix death-knowledge into a worked object requires a bridge that you do not yet have the words for. The ink is on the shaft. The wax is at the socket. Huginn has not moved. The fire is good and the night is good and Pell is watching without comment — but the ceremony reaches its crux and finds no anchor. The marks are craft, not binding. The spear is a fine spear. It is not an artifact.

turns.player: GAMBIT — "Bind the spear to death and to Huginn — a true death-bound weapon, made to grow."
turns.summary: Rune-binding ceremony completed rough — death-register opened in the blade, Pell's maker's mark seated, Huginn's thread present but nascent. The spear has begun.
turns.narration:
The fire is honest now — hardwood, chest-high, no roof above it but cold stars. You begin with both abilities open at once, Deathsense and Order-Sense running together for the first time, and the first thing you feel is the friction. The two readings do not want to share a channel. Order-Sense pulls toward the spear's structure — grain, flex, load — while Deathsense reaches past that into something older, the particular quiet of iron that has been shaped by heat and struck and cooled, the faint record of every edge that ever passed through something living. For a moment they fight, and you get noise: the shaft's grain scrambles, the death-reading fragments. You let out a slow breath and stop trying to hold them apart. You stop trying to hold them together either. You just let both things be present. It does not resolve cleanly. What you get is a map with two layers, slightly out of register — the load-bearing seam of the wood and the death-register of the iron speaking in different languages. Rough. But readable.

Then the preparation hits, and it hits like a door opening. You have worked ceremonies before — not this one, not this specific thing, but the shape of it — and something in tonight's conditions is exactly right. The open sky. The hardwood smell. Pell seated without ceremony, mug in both hands, watching with the same quality she gives ironwork. Huginn on the stone ring three inches from the blade, not moving. You are not performing. You press the cooper's wax into the socket throat with your thumb and you do not think about the watching — you think about the flex point eighteen inches down, where the grain gives without breaking, and you mark it first, a long smeared stroke of pitch-ink along the spine, following the grain's own logic. The wood takes it like it was waiting. You feel the two-layered reading underneath your hands and you stop fighting the double vision and let it guide you: where the death-register thickens, you press the mark deeper; where the structure is load-bearing, you follow the wood's own line. The runes are not decorative. They land where the spear already has opinion about what it is.

The blade resists you at first — iron older than the ash, a different grain entirely, its death-register dry and dense and not interested in being addressed. You stop. Look at Pell. She has been watching this whole time without a word, but when your eye finds her she does not wait: she sets the mug down on the wall, stands, crosses the yard in three steps, and crouches beside you at the stone ring. She does not ask what you need. She takes the pitch-ink from your hand and turns the spear-tip in her fingers the way she would examine her own work — checking the socket seam, the shoulder, the edge grind — and then she presses her own mark into the flat of the blade below the socket, one clean stroke, the kind a smith leaves when the work is finished and accounted for. Her maker's mark. The iron accepts it. The death-register in the blade opens a fraction, not wide, but enough — something in the iron recognizes the hand that shaped it, and the rune you press in beside her mark settles instead of skating. Huginn shifts on the stone ring. Not much. One foot, repositioning. The pale eye moves to the blade and stays there.

You finish the binding with both readings still running, still slightly out of register, and what you get at the end is not what you aimed for exactly. It is not a perfectly tuned artifact. The runes hold — they are in the wood and the iron and they are load-bearing, not decorative, pressed into the places where the spear already knows what it is. The death-register is open in the blade: it will know when it ends something, and that knowing will accumulate. The bond to Huginn is present but nascent — a thread, not a cord, woven through the pale eye's long attention and the bird's motionless hour at the stone ring. The spear is not finished becoming what it will be. But it has begun. The fire burns down a little, settling into coals, and the yard is quiet and warm and smells of pitch and hardwood, and Pell is still crouching beside you with her hands resting on her knees, looking at the blade.

turns.player: Tell Pell plainly what the ceremony was for — that the spear is meant to grow into something, and that her mark is load-bearing in ways that go beyond ironwork. See how she takes that.
turns.summary: Silas tells Pell plainly that her maker's mark is load-bearing in the death-binding — she takes it in, does not disbelieve, and asks what 'grows' means practically.
turns.narration:
The coals pulse low and orange, and the forge yard is quiet enough that you can hear the river two streets over. You pick up your mug, let the warmth of it settle into your hands, and tell her straight.

"The mark you pressed in — it's not decoration, and it's not just proof of make. The death-reading I was working is keyed to endings. What I was trying to build is a weapon that knows when something is dying, that can find the seam between a life still held and one already released, and work there. Your mark is in the iron now. That means the iron answers to you as much as to me — maybe more, at the moment. That's not a metaphor. If this spear grows into what it's meant to be, you'll have a stake in what it becomes."

Huginn lifts one foot from the stone ring and sets it down again. That's all.

Pell does not look away from the blade. She picks up her mug, drinks, sets it back on the wall. A long pause. Not discomfort — more the silence of someone taking an accurate measurement before they speak. When she does, her voice is even, the same register she'd use discussing the temper of a particular steel. "I've put my mark in a lot of iron," she says. "Never had anyone tell me it meant something after." Another beat. "I'm not sure I don't believe you." Her eyes come up to yours, level, unhurried. "Tell me what 'grows into' means. Practically."

turns.player: Tell her the truth of what you don't know yet — the spear is a beginning, not a finished thing, and you'll only learn what it becomes by using it and watching what it does.
turns.summary: Silas tells Pell plainly the spear is a beginning — its nature gets answered by use. She files it and confirms the morning plan.
turns.narration:
The coals have settled low enough that the fire gives more warmth than light now. You hold the mug in both hands and look at Pell across the pit.

"Honestly? I don't know what it grows into. That's not a dodge — it's the actual condition. The binding is rough. There's a thread to Huginn, there's your mark, there's the death-register opened in the blade. What those three things become together I'll only learn by carrying it north tomorrow, by using it on something that's dying, and watching what it does."

Huginn shifts one foot on the stone ring and makes no sound.

"That's the honest answer to your question," you say. "The spear is a beginning. It has the right bones in it, and the right hands made it, and I trust the direction. The rest gets answered in the field."

Pell turns her mug once between her palms — a slow rotation, thinking without hurrying it. She looks at the blade where her mark sits in the iron, then back at you. "Right," she says, and it lands less like acceptance and more like someone filing a fact in the correct place. Not satisfied, not unsatisfied — accurate. She takes a drink. "North gate, before the light gets high. Corvin'll be there first."

The coals tick and settle. The forge yard is quiet, the river just audible two streets over.

turns.player: Thank both Pell and Huginn for their participation - and find your britches to put on.  Ask Pell what she thought of the ritual.
turns.summary: Silas thanks Pell and Huginn after the ceremony; Pell says pressing her mark felt like the iron was already waiting for her hand.
turns.narration:
You find your clothes where you left them — folded on the south wall, away from the fire's heat — and dress without ceremony. The night air is still cold enough that the linen feels good once it's on. You take your mug back up.

"Thank you," you say, to both of them. You mean it without performance. Huginn receives it with one slow blink and shifts to face away from the fire, apparently satisfied. Pell lifts her mug a fraction — acknowledgment, not toast.

Then you ask her what she thought of it.

She's quiet for a moment, turning the mug in her hands. The coals give just enough light to read her face: not troubled, not closed. Working through something. "Stranger than I expected," she says finally. "And I expected strange." She glances toward the blade, where the last of the pitch-ink has dried into the grain. "When I pressed the mark in — felt like the iron was already waiting for it. That hasn't happened before. Iron waits for heat. It doesn't wait for a hand." She takes a drink. "Don't know what to call that. But it happened, so." Her shoulders settle. "Good bones in it. Like you said."

turns.player: It was waiting for a different part of you - I see that part too... it's beautiful.  You have a gift... I could teach you some of what I know if you'd like to spend more time with me. :)
turns.summary: Silas offers intimate mentorship to Pell; she receives it with guarded interest and says yes — after the hunt, at the forge.
turns.narration:
You say it quietly, without preamble. "It was waiting for a different part of you. I see that part too." You pause, let that sit. The coals give just enough light that her face is readable — the way it stills when something lands that she wasn't braced for. "It's beautiful, honestly. The way the iron answered you." 

Pell turns her mug once in her hands. She doesn't deflect and she doesn't lean in — she holds the compliment at arm's length first, the way she holds anything new, turning it to find the weight of it. Then she looks up at you with a directness that is entirely hers. "I've been working iron since I was eleven," she says. "It's never waited for me before. It gets hot and it moves. That's different from waiting."

"I know," you say. "That's what I mean."

Another beat. Huginn is facing the dark yard, not watching, which is somehow more attentive than watching. Pell glances at the spear where it rests along the stone ring — the runes faint in the low light, her mark invisible inside the blade but present. When she looks back, something in her expression has shifted: not softened, exactly, but opened a fraction, the way a door opens when the latch gives.

"You're offering to teach me something," she says. It is not quite a question. She is not quite agreeing. But there is no refusal in it either — just Pell doing what Pell does, which is measure before committing. "After the hunt. At the forge. You can show me what you were doing with the grain." A pause. "And whatever the other part is — the part I apparently have — we can see whether it's real in daylight or only real at the fire."

turns.player: Leave it there — the offer stands, she's heard it, and there's a hunt in the morning. Bank the fire and let the night close.
turns.summary: Silas banks the fire, the offer stands, and the three of them settle into the night before the hunt.
turns.narration:
You don't push it further. The offer is out there, she's taken it in, and the night is doing its work — the kind of quiet that settles after something real has been said. You set your mug on the stone ring and crouch to bank the fire, pressing the coals together with the heel of the poker and covering the edge with a thin layer of ash to hold the warmth through till morning. The orange drops to a deep, slow red.

Pell watches you do it without comment. It's a forge-keeper's job and you're doing it correctly, and that registers with her in the same register everything else does — practical first, then the rest. She drains the last of her mug and sets it on the south wall beside the split wood. "North gate," she says. "Before the light gets high."

"Before the light gets high," you confirm.

Huginn drops from the stone ring to the ground without sound and walks — actually walks, two deliberate steps — to a spot just beyond the pit's warmth. It folds itself down into the loose posture of a bird that intends to stay until morning. Not a vigil. Just the attended quiet of something that has nowhere else to be.

You take the assembled spear off the ring and lean it against the south wall within reach of where you'll lay your bedroll, and then you let the night close around the three of you — the coals breathing low, the stars clear overhead, the forge dark and cool behind.

turns.player: Before sleep, sit with the spear a moment and run Order-Sense along it — read what the binding looks like now that the fire is low and the ceremony is settled
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the death-binding — settled, clean, Pell's marks structural; Huginn's thread nascent but oriented.
turns.narration:
You pick up the spear before you sit — not to use it, just to hold it. The ash shaft is cool from the night air, the road lashing tight under your fingers. You settle on the stone ring with it across your knees and let Order-Sense run through quietly, the way you'd run a thumb along a seam to check it holds.

What you find is cleaner than you expected.

The binding has settled into the wood and iron the way pitch settles into grain — not sitting on top of it, but in it. The death-register in the blade is narrow and specific, a single thread of attention rather than a broad inscription, and it has already oriented itself: toward Huginn, toward you, toward the north where you'll walk tomorrow. You can feel the load-bearing points Pell's mark established — two of them, one at the socket throat and one at the secondary flex near the branch-bud knot — and they are structural in a way yours alone would not have been. The iron held her hand because there was already something in her the iron recognized. The binding confirms it without explaining it.

The one rough edge: the thread to Huginn is nascent, not seated. It knows its direction but has not been tested by use. That will settle or it won't — and you can't read which from here. You set the spear back against the south wall, next to where Huginn is tucked and dark, and let it rest.

turns.player: Spend a few minutes with the Rune-Craft Kit tightening the nascent thread to Huginn before the night fully closes — while the reading is fresh.
turns.summary: Silas deepens the Huginn-thread in the spear's death-binding — a critical tightening that takes the connection from nascent to present.
turns.narration:
You don't put the spear down. The reading is still fresh in your hands — the thread to Huginn oriented but thin, a single filament where you want a cable. You pick up the Rune-Craft Kit from beside the stone ring: the wax stub, the last of the pitch-ink, the folded leather strip. Pell watches without comment. She's tired, but she's the kind of tired that keeps one eye open.

You press the wax along the base of the binding just above the socket throat — not a new mark, an extension of the existing one, a follow-through stroke that deepens the groove the inscription already found. The pitch-ink goes in after it, thin and black, worked into the grain with the leather strip. You're not writing anything new. You're listening to what's already there and saying it more clearly.

What happens next is quiet and entirely real. The thread pulls taut. Not toward you — toward the dark beyond the pit, where Huginn sits folded and still. Its one open eye sharpens. It lifts its head, a short precise movement, and for a moment the coals throw a line of orange across the blade that has no business landing that way, at that angle, with the fire this low. The thread in the binding goes from nascent to present — not a tether, not a leash, but something like a name spoken between two people who already knew each other's faces. A mutual registration.

Huginn settles again. One slow blink. It does not croak. But the silence after has a different texture than the silence before, and you can feel the difference in the shaft under your hands — the spear knows what it is adjacent to now. The binding is still rough, still a beginning. But the beginning has teeth. Pell exhales through her nose. 'Right,' she says, with the flat affect of a woman updating a mental ledger. 'Morning, then.'

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 3: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

*✦ A new portrait captures who you've become.*

turns.player: Use Deathsense on the spear — now that the thread is taut, read whether the binding has an orientation toward anything already dying in the north wood.
turns.summary: Deathsense reads north through the spear but the forge yard's saturation blurs the signal — something is out there, not tonight, not specific enough to name.
turns.narration:
The night is very still. You set the spear across your knees again — the pitch-ink barely dry, the wax still faintly warm at the socket throat — and let Deathsense open, not wide but directed, running the thread out along the binding the way you'd follow a rope into dark water.

The forge yard resists you. Not hostility — more like the place has too much iron in it, too many things that were shaped by ending and are now simply objects. The smokehouse smell carries on the light wind from the north. Dead fish, cured meat, the specific flatness of things already processed past the point where Deathsense finds purchase. The place has been a site of finished deaths for decades and they all blur together into a low, undifferentiated hum that the binding can't sort through cleanly.

You get something. North — that much is real, the thread does pull that direction, and there's a texture to it: more than one, not immediate, not tonight. The sounder you already know about, probably. But whether that's all of it, whether one of those animals is already tipped toward dying in the way Deathsense reads clearly — you can't say. The interference is too thick here, the yard too saturated with old endings. You'd need to be closer, or somewhere the air hasn't been marinating in cured flesh for twenty years.

Huginn shifts on his perch beyond the pit, both eyes open, watching the direction your attention just went. He doesn't croak. He tilts his head a few degrees north and holds it.

Pell's voice comes from the south wall, low and unimpressed. 'You're doing something else.'

turns.player: Ask Pella directly - does she want you to sleep in the yard, or...
turns.summary: Silas asks Pell about sleeping arrangements; she invites him inside without ceremony.
turns.narration:
The coals have dropped to a steady red, barely enough to read the yard by. Huginn has not moved — still tilted north, that slight hold in the neck that means he's working on something. You set the spear down against the stone ring carefully, blade pointing away from both of you, and look across at Pell.

"Does it make more sense for me to sleep in the yard, or—" You leave it there. Not pressing. She knows the rest of the question.

Pell picks up her mug, finds it empty, sets it back down. She's quiet for a count of three — not hesitating, just giving it the weight it deserves. "Forge floor's dry and out of the wind," she says finally. "There's a folded canvas behind the bellows. Cleaner than the yard." A beat. "I didn't say you were sleeping alone on it."

She pushes off the wall, takes both mugs inside. The forge door stays open behind her.

turns.player: Follow her in — take what the night is offering
turns.summary: Silas follows Pell into the forge for the night — spear against the wall, canvas down, Huginn still oriented north in the yard.
turns.narration:
You pick up the spear and follow her in.

The forge is warm in the way a room is warm when the day's heat has been banked rather than lost — the big stone hearth still radiating through the far wall, the smell of coal-dust and iron-scale and something underneath that is just old wood. Pell sets the two mugs on the workbench without looking at you and moves to the far corner where a folded canvas sits behind the bellows housing, thick and a little stiff, the kind of thing that has served as a bed before. She drops it at the near side of the hearth without ceremony, unfolds it partway with her foot, and turns back to her bench. Not pointed. Just done.

You lean the spear against the wall beside the door — close, blade up, within arm's reach of where you'll sleep. The rope and the pack come off your back. Through the open door the yard is a dark rectangle, and Huginn is a slightly darker shape at its edge, still tilted north, still holding. You watch him a moment. He doesn't turn. Whatever he's listening to, it hasn't resolved itself yet.

Pell sits on the edge of her own bedroll in the back corner — a proper one, wool-faced, clearly years old — and pulls one boot off. She glances at you, just once, the way someone does when they want to confirm the room is still in order. It is. She sets the boot down beside the other one, lies back, and closes her eyes. The forge settles around you both: a low tick of cooling iron, the slow breath of the banked coals through the grate, the darkness outside the door that smells of early spring and the faint iron of the river.

turns.player: Take off my own boots and most of my clothes... Lie down for a bit, then ask Pell about her dreams of the future.
turns.summary: Silas and Pell settle into the forge for the night — a quiet exchange about the future before sleep.
turns.narration:
You sit on the edge of the canvas and work your boots off — road-stiff leather, the laces still faintly damp at the toe from the morning grass. You set them beside the spear, sole to sole, and peel off your outer layer and the work shirt beneath it, folding both onto the pack. The forge is warm enough that it doesn't matter. The canvas is thick and slightly rough and when you lie back on it the residual heat from the hearth comes at you low and even, a pressure more than a warmth.

For a while neither of you says anything. Pell is on her bedroll in the back corner, boots off, eyes closed — or closed enough. Huginn is still in the yard. The coals breathe faintly through the far wall. You can hear the river somewhere below the village, and you realize it's the first time today you've stopped moving long enough to hear it.

After a bit you say, without looking over: "What does this look like in five years? The forge, I mean. Or whatever comes after it."

There's a pause. Not a sleepy one. "Keep the forge," she says, flat and certain. Then, quieter: "Maybe something else alongside it. I've been thinking about the iron more than usual lately." She doesn't explain what that means, and you don't push — the day she presses her mark into a blade that was already waiting for her hand is probably not the day she has language for what she's reaching toward. "You?" The question comes back without inflection, genuinely curious.

The fire settles. A coal shifts with a dry tick. You have a hunt in the morning and the north wood has more than one presence in it, and it occurs to you that the question she's asking is one you haven't answered for yourself in longer than you'd like to admit.

turns.player: Tell her what you actually know — that the short way you took brought you here, and 'here' keeps turning out to be somewhere that matters. That's as close to a plan as you've got.
turns.summary: Silas tells Pell about the short way and the vanished path — she listens, then turns the question back on him.
turns.narration:
The coals are down to a slow red pulse. You can hear the river from here — faint, the way water sounds when the night has gone still around it.

"The short way," you say, looking up at the ceiling beams. "I took it. Didn't know it was the short way at the time — I thought I was cutting across something that didn't need to be walked all the way through." A breath. "The path behind me was gone when I turned around. Not overgrown. Gone."

Pell doesn't say anything. The silence means she's listening.

"Since then I've been finding that the places I end up — they tend to be places that needed someone there. Not me specifically. Just someone who can see a certain kind of thing." You turn your head toward her corner. She's on her side now, facing the wall or facing away, you can't quite tell in the low light. "I don't have a destination. I have a direction. North, lately. The spear reads north." You pause. "And here turned out to matter. So."

"That's not nothing," Pell says after a moment. Not consoling — just observational, the way she'd note that a blade had good temper. She shifts on her bedroll, and then she says it quietly: "You?"

The question sits in the warm dark. She's already given her own version. This is her asking what yours looks like when you say it plain.

turns.player: Don't answer in words — reach across the dark and let that be the answer.
turns.summary: Silas reaches across the dark; Pell's hand meets his briefly before she turns back to sleep.
turns.narration:
Your hand moves before the words do. You reach across the dark — not far, just past the edge of your canvas, palm up, offered — and let it lie there between you.

Pell is quiet for a moment. Then her hand finds yours. Not a grip, not a press — just the weight of it, fingers settling, the way you'd rest your palm on a workbench to feel if the iron has cooled. Her hand is calloused at the base of every finger and warm from the bedroll, and she doesn't pull you toward her or move herself toward you. She just stays.

A log shifts somewhere deep in the banked hearth. Huginn makes no sound from the yard.

She lets go after a while — unhurried, the way she does most things — and turns back onto her side. "Before the light gets high," she says quietly. Not cold. Just true. The hunt is tomorrow and she intends to be ready for it, and this is how she holds both things at once without choosing between them.

You lie on your back in the dark, the forge still warm, the spear a shape against the wall. Sleep comes in pieces.

turns.player: I want to use the runic bound spear (the one in my inventory needs a new description reflecting this) to connect with Huginn outside to make sure nothing is moving around in the yard.
turns.summary: Silas attempts to use the death-bound spear as a runic relay for Huginn — the working finds the edge of what palework can do and stops there.
turns.narration:
You lie still and reach for the spear — not with your hand, not yet. You let your attention travel the binding, the thread you pressed into wax and pitch-ink last night at the south pit, the one that runs from shaft to blade to Huginn's particular awareness of endings. A quiet working, barely a working at all: just a listening.

The thread is there. You can feel it — or feel its absence properly, which is close enough. But the conduit doesn't open. Palework knows the dying and the dead; it reads endings already underway. What you're asking it to do now is different — use the binding as a wire, route the sensing outward through iron and binding-cord into the yard, check what moves there. The power obliges as far as it can and then stops, cleanly, the way a line goes taut before it parts. The spear is a bound object, not a relay. Huginn is a witness, not an ear you can borrow.

You get nothing useful about the yard. What you get instead is the shape of the working you tried — a clean sense of where palework ends and something else would have to begin. The thread to Huginn is present and mutual; it simply doesn't run in that direction. The forge is warm. The coals pulse. Outside, Huginn has not moved.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense instead — read whether Huginn's stillness has a shape, a pattern to it
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads Huginn's vigil as patterned and calm — something north cycles at a steady interval, present but not approaching.
turns.narration:
You let Palework go and come at it differently. Order-Sense costs less and asks less — it doesn't need a wire between you and Huginn, only the ability to read what's already there.

You open it quietly. CONSERVE: a small expenditure, a shallow read. What you find is this: Huginn's stillness has grain. It is not the random stillness of a bird roosting or a bird sleeping. There is a regularity to it — the angle of the head, the periodic slow blink at a fixed interval, the way his weight is distributed forward rather than settled. He is watching something. Not reacting, not alarmed, not readying to move. He is tracking a pattern that repeats at intervals and has not yet changed. Whatever is north, it is not coming closer. It is present and it is cycling — doing the same thing, at roughly the same tempo, over and over again.

Pattern without urgency. Vigil, not alarm.

You let the sense close. The forge is warm. Pell's breathing is slow and even behind you. The coals give their low red pulse. You've learned what you needed: Huginn is a watchman on a quiet wall, and the night is not going to move before morning.

turns.player: Sleep. The vigil is Huginn's; you've confirmed it's holding. Close your eyes.
turns.summary: Silas sleeps through the last of the night; Pell and Corvin are already moving in the pre-dawn yard.
turns.narration:
You close your eyes.

The hearth pulses slow and red through your eyelids. The forge smells of iron and char and the faint lanolin-ghost of the oiled leather binding still curing on the spear shaft. Huginn makes no sound from the yard — just the occasional soft weight-shift, the click of a talon on stone at his fixed interval. You let that be enough. The pattern holds. Nothing comes.

Sleep arrives without ceremony, the way it does when the body has earned it: a long day of knife-work and rune-pressing and walking east and walking back, and now the canvas and the warm dark and the even sound of Pell's breathing from across the room. You don't dream, or if you do, nothing of it stays. Just the slow red pulse and the smell of the fire going down.

When you surface it is still dark, but a different dark — thinner, the kind that has light somewhere behind it. Huginn has moved: he's on the door-lintel now, looking in, and the yard beyond him is the color of cold ash. Pell's bedroll is empty. The assembled spear leans where you left it. Somewhere outside, faint and close, boots on hard ground — two sets, then three. A voice, low: Corvin, asking something about the north path. Pell answering in two syllables. The hunt is ready.

turns.player: Pause at the door-lintel — check in with Huginn before stepping out. See if his read of north has sharpened overnight.
turns.summary: Silas checks in with Huginn at the forge door — north holds two or three presences, patterned and still. Hunt departs.
turns.narration:
You sit up from the canvas, the forge interior grey and still around you. The hearth has gone to ash-white coals, barely warm. You pull on your shirt, lace your boots, and cross the few steps to the door-lintel where Huginn sits, close enough to touch.

You open Order-Sense — a shallow cast, just enough to read what he's holding. The forge works against you slightly: decades of iron-work, the banked char, the particular density of a space that has processed so much metal and heat, all of it layering a low static over anything finer. You push gently past it.

Huginn's vigil has the same grain as last night — patterned, intervals intact, nothing urgent. But you're closer now, and the pattern yields one thing more: whatever cycles north is not a single presence. Two sources, maybe three, each with its own cadence, loosely synchronized the way animals sharing territory will adjust to each other without meaning to. Alive. Not approaching. Not retreating. Holding ground.

Huginn turns his head and regards you with the calm eye. He does not croak. His stillness is a plain answer: *still there, still fine, your call.*

In the yard behind you, Pell says something low to Corvin. A boot scrapes gravel. The light is the color of river-water, thin and honest, and it is time to go.

turns.player: Shoulder the Assembled Mid-Weight Spear, whistle Huginn off the lintel, and lead out — let the pace set itself once you clear the north gate.
turns.summary: Hunt party clears the north gate at pre-dawn — Huginn orienting north, spear shouldered, party moving in natural order into the meadow.
turns.narration:
You lift the Assembled Mid-Weight Spear from where it leans against the doorframe — the ash shaft cold and smooth in your palm, the lashing tight under your fingers, the iron tip catching the thin pre-dawn gray. The weight settles across your shoulder the way it should. You whistle once, low and flat.

Huginn drops from the lintel without ceremony. One wingbeat, two, and he's up over the forge roof and angling north — not racing ahead, just orienting, the way a compass needle finds its bearing and rests. Pell clocks the motion from the yard and shoulders her own kit without a word. Corvin turns from whatever he and Pell were discussing, takes in the spear and the bird and Silas in the door, and gives a single nod that means *ready*.

You cross the yard. Gravel under your boots, the cold a clean bite after the forge's lingering warmth. The village is quiet — a dog somewhere to the south, a cookfire smell from the east lane, the river's sound low and constant past the gardens. The four of you fall into a natural order on the north track: Corvin out front by half a step because he knows the fence-lines, Pell just behind his left shoulder, you behind her, Huginn a grey shape moving above the treeline at intervals. The light is building but not yet arrived — that particular pre-dawn blue where shapes are clear but color hasn't come back into the world yet.

The north gate is a gap in a dry-stone wall, no hinge or bar, just a marked threshold where the village becomes the meadow. You pass through it and the ground softens underfoot, dew-wet grass replacing gravel. Huginn circles once overhead and then drives north along the tree-line, settling into his interval. Whatever held its ground there through the night is still holding it.

turns.player: Ask Corvin what he knows about this stretch of the north wood — he farms the east furlong, he's been watching the ground longer than you have.
turns.summary: Corvin briefs the party on boar ground and river-smell displacement — something large moves in the wet scrub ahead.
turns.narration:
Corvin doesn't slow down. He answers the way he does most things — without preamble, eyes on the ground ahead.

"Boar work the wet strip along the fence-line, maybe a quarter mile in. Come out mornings to root the sedge, back under the canopy by midday." He steps over a tussock, finds the worn trace of a game trail, and settles onto it without breaking stride. "Sounder's been at it three seasons now — a sow and last year's young, mostly. I've counted five on a good morning, four reliable. Bull's been around but he's not fixed to them." A pause, long enough to be deliberate. "River smell's been carrying north on the low air. They've been pushing their rooting ground higher these past weeks — away from the streamside sedge, toward the drier scrub. Not like them."

He says that last part flat, not alarmed, just noting it. The way he'd note a fence-post leaning wrong — something to watch.

Pell speaks from just behind your left shoulder, voice low. "Same direction we're heading." She isn't asking.

The game trail ahead narrows and bends northwest around a stand of wet willows, the bark pale in the early light. The tree-line proper is fifty yards ahead — the canopy still bare enough that the sky shows through in grey panels. Huginn is already past it, a dark shape threading between trunks before vanishing. His interval is unchanged: regular, calm, holding north.

Somewhere in the middle distance, something large shifts its weight in wet ground. You hear it clearly — a single soft percussion, roots and mud. Then quiet.

turns.player: Read the mud ahead — crouch at the trail's edge and use Order-Sense to find the pattern beneath the ground sign: how many animals, which direction, how recently
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the mud — sow and young twenty minutes ahead, something heavy passed through last night and moved on.
turns.narration:
The squall comes from nowhere — a cold gust off the heights that turns the pre-dawn blue to something grayer, drives a thin spatter of rain sideways across the meadow for thirty seconds, and then stops. Not a storm; just the valley changing its mind. The wet grass shivers once and goes still. Corvin glances up, reads it, goes back to walking. Pell turns her collar up.

You crouch at the trail's edge where the ground softens — a yard of bare mud between the game trace and the first sedge tussocks, boot-chewed by animals coming and going. You let Order-Sense settle rather than push it: a light touch, palms near the surface, reading what's already there.

The pattern comes up clearly enough. Three distinct disturbance signatures — spaced the way a sounder spreads when it's feeding, not fleeing. The near one is the freshest: a sow's broad front trotter, the dew-edge still sharp, twenty minutes at most. The other two trail her by an hour or more, made last night in softer ground that has since firmed up slightly. One of those older prints has the weight behind it of something heavier — the bull, or something close to a bull's mass. He came through, but he didn't stay. The sow is still in the sedge strip ahead; the young haven't broken from her yet.

You stand and look at Corvin. "Sow's close. Twenty minutes, maybe less. Came through here leading — the young are still with her. Something heavier came last night and didn't stay." You keep your voice level, the way you'd note a fence-post that needs attention.

turns.player: Read the heavier track more closely with Order-Sense — the bull came through and didn't stay; find out where it went
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the bull's track — northwest, unhurried, destination unclear. Sow and young still ahead.
turns.narration:
You keep the crouch, palms still near the mud, and let Order-Sense narrow onto the heavier prints — the two with bull-weight, dew-edge softer than the sow's, made somewhere between midnight and first light. The pattern is there but it doesn't resolve cleanly at this range. You get direction: northwest, angling away from the sedge strip, toward the drier scrub on the far side of the fence-line. You get weight — heavy, deliberate, unhurried. What you don't get is a terminus. The trail bends into the canopy and the pattern thins out before it gives you a destination. He moved on. Where he stopped, you can't say.

You stand and wipe your hands on your thigh. Huginn is still threading north between bare trunks, interval unchanged — whatever he's orienting on is ahead and separate from the bull's line, which means at least for now the two aren't the same problem.

Corvin is already reading your posture. He doesn't ask — he just shifts his grip on the light spear and tilts his head a fraction west, offering the question without words. Pell holds where she is, eyes on the tree-line, letting you speak when you're ready.

turns.player: Tell Corvin and Pell what you read — bull went northwest, sow is twenty minutes ahead — and let the three of you agree on a spread before you enter the tree-line
turns.summary: Hunt party agrees on a spread and enters the tree-line — sow and young in the sedge strip ahead.
turns.narration:
You stand and give them the short version. "Bull went northwest, maybe midnight. Unhurried — not spooked, just moving to higher ground. Sow and young are still in the sedge strip ahead. Twenty minutes, maybe less now." You nod toward the tree-line where Huginn's grey shape is threading between bare trunks, heading a few degrees east of the bull's line. "Huginn's still orienting on something separate. Two or three presences, patterned. North, not northwest."

Corvin chews on that for a moment, then looks at the canopy gap. "If we take the bull we take him alone, in drier scrub — better footing, less noise. Sow's more predictable. Young with her means she won't break early, but it means she won't yield easy either." He's not advocating. He's handing Silas the variables.

Pell turns from the tree-line to look at you both. "Sow's the safer kill. Bull's the cleaner one." A pause, flat and practical. "Spread: you and the spear on the inside, cut her line to the sedge. I take the east flank and keep the young moving forward. Corvin closes the west and we pin her against the fence-line." Her eyes go to Corvin. He gives a short nod — he's walked that fence-line a hundred times; he knows exactly where it bends.

The three of you slip into the tree-line without ceremony, footfalls going quiet on the wet leaf-mat. Huginn drops out of sight somewhere ahead, unhurried. The sedge strip is a dark line between bare trunks, maybe a hundred yards in, and from here you can already catch the low earthy smell of rooted mud.

turns.player: Press the approach — move into position and close the last hundred yards quietly, trusting the spread you've set
turns.summary: Hunt party closes to forty yards before the sow catches wind — she's rooted and alert, facing Silas, thirty seconds to commitment.
turns.narration:
You move first, spear low, weight shifting heel-to-toe on the wet leaf-mat. Pell peels east without a word — you catch the pale line of her collar disappearing between two birches. Corvin takes the west fence-line, staying inside the shadow of the trunks where the ground is drier. For a hundred yards nobody breathes louder than they have to.

You close to forty yards before the sedge strip gives you up. Not a sound — just the wind's direction going sideways for a moment, carrying your scent ahead of you on a cold eddy off the heights. The sow catches it. You hear her grunt once, low and fast, and the rustle of piglets bunching. She doesn't bolt. She turns to face your direction and goes still, dark mass in the grey sedge, and you can see the pale glint of one eye.

You stop. The party stops. The sow holds her ground — this is her strip, she knows it, and she's not certain enough of you yet to commit to a direction. But she's rooted now, alert, ribs tightening with each breath. You have maybe thirty seconds before she decides. To your east, somewhere in the birches, Pell's position is good — she hasn't been scented. To the west, Corvin has the fence-line at his back. The spread is solid. But you're the one she's looking at, and the initiative is yours to spend or hold.

Huginn is silent ahead. No croak, no wing-beat. Whatever the bird is tracking north of the strip, it isn't reacting to this. That's its own kind of note.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense — a light read on the sow's pattern right now, the way her weight is loaded, which direction her feet are set. Find the pivot before she takes it.
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the sow's weight — she's loaded to bolt northwest through the fence-gap, not charge.
turns.narration:
You let Order-Sense in small — a conserved read, barely a flicker, just enough to feel the weight distribution under the sow's stillness. The north wood resists it, though; the birch roots and the wet mat are too tangled, too many separate living systems overlapping, and for a half-second you get noise before you get signal. Then the pattern resolves.

She's loaded on her front legs. Weight forward, snout down a fraction, feet set wide and slightly canted to her right — your left. That's the bolt direction. A charge would have her coiled the other way, hindquarters low, and she isn't coiling. She's wound to break for the fence-gap, northwest, same corridor the bull used. She's going to try to take the young with her through that gap, and she's about ten seconds from deciding you're close enough to force the issue.

You have the pivot. Now you need to use it.

turns.player: Signal Pell with a low click — two fast, one slow — and let her east position drive the sow back toward the centre of the strip. You hold ground and let the spread do the work.
turns.summary: Pell's flank closes the sow's bolt corridor — spread holds, sow turned and centred, hunt at the knife-edge.
turns.narration:
The click leaves your teeth before conscious thought — two fast, one slow, low enough that the sow's ears don't track it but Pell's position does. You hear the answer: a single heel-plant in the birch-leaf, deliberate, and then Pell steps out of the tree-line at an angle, unhurried, arms slightly wide, coming in from the east and a little north of the sow's bolt line. No rush. No noise past the soft give of wet ground.

The sow swings her head. She had the gap, she knew she had the gap, and now there is something between her and it — not close, not threatening, just there, blocking the geometry she'd already committed to. You feel the decision in her weight before she makes it: the northwest bolt dies. Her feet shuffle. She cants back toward centre, snout working, trying to re-read the situation. The piglets bunch tighter. She is not calm, but she is held.

You bring the spear up, not high — waist-level, blade forward, weight settled over your back foot. The sow is square to you now, forty yards, open sedge between you. Corvin is on the west fence-line with the corridor closed. Pell is east and steady. Huginn is somewhere north in the grey canopy, still moving, not yet stopped. The spread has done its work. What you do in the next few seconds is yours alone.

turns.player: GAMBIT — Safely kill the Sow - try to have Corvin get the kill, but use skills to ensure the kill is clean
turns.summary: Sow killed cleanly — Corvin's strike, Silas's bar, clean finish. Huginn attends. The north wood goes quiet.
turns.narration:
You shift the spear to your left hand and give Corvin the close-signal — a flat palm dropped once toward the sedge. He reads it without hesitation. The fence-line shadow releases him and he moves in from the west at a walk, not a run, weight low, the modified light spear held two-handed across his body. The sow hears him. Her head swings west. That is all you needed.

Corvin reaches the strip's edge and plants his feet. The thrust is clean — aimed low behind the shoulder, short-range, no theatre. The sow kicks hard and turns into the wound instead of away from it, which is what they do, and Corvin holds his ground and lets her run herself onto the blade rather than fight her off it. You are already moving to his left, spear forward, ready to cross if she breaks. She doesn't break. She goes to her knees in the sedge four feet from where she started, and the piglets scatter north into the strip.

Then the third step happens without you having to decide it: you feel the sow's weight shift through your feet a half-second before she would have made a last push toward Pell's position, and you step in from the east side and lay the Deathbound Runic Spear flat across the back of her neck — not a killing strike, just a bar of weight and presence. She stills under it. The death-register in the blade is already reading her. You hold position, spear horizontal, and let Corvin finish the work with his knife — one cut, placed right. It is over before the grey light has climbed another inch up the birch trunks.

Huginn lands on the fence-post above Corvin's left shoulder, unbidden. It looks at the sow for exactly the time it takes to confirm the ending, then tucks its beak and says nothing. Pell comes in from the east, arms dropping, and stops two paces away. The north wood settles back into its own sound — dripping canopy, the faint creak of the fence-line, piglets moving through the sedge somewhere to the north.

turns.player: Say something to Corvin — acknowledge the strike. Let the moment land between the three of you before the work begins.
turns.summary: Sow dressed and acknowledged — Corvin's strike named, Pell flags the northward convergence, Huginn points the same way.
turns.narration:
The sow is still. Corvin hasn't moved yet — knife hand low, weight settled, the way a man finishes a thing properly before he stands up from it.

You say it quietly: "Good strike." Not praise exactly, more like naming what happened. The kind of thing you say when the work was done right and saying nothing would be its own comment.

Corvin exhales through his nose. Nods once, chin dipping toward the sedge. He sets his palm flat against the sow's flank for a moment — a brief, plain acknowledgment — then draws the knife and gets to work. No ceremony. The gesture is ceremony enough.

Pell hasn't spoken. She's still a pace east of the tree-line, arms loose at her sides, watching Huginn on the fence-post. The bird hasn't moved since the sow went down. Beak tucked, one eye tracking the north strip where the piglets went. After a moment Pell says, level and quiet: "Bull went the same direction."

It isn't a question. It's the thing she's been turning over since you read the track at the tree-line, and she's naming it now that the immediate work is done. Her gaze moves from Huginn to the sedge north of you — the direction the piglets scattered, the direction Huginn was threading before the kill, the direction the bull moved overnight. Three separate signals pointing the same way.

Huginn shifts on the post. One slow rotation of the head, beak angling north. It doesn't croak. It doesn't need to.

turns.player: Tell Corvin to continue to process the sow, you're taking your date on to find the bull. There's more hunt ahead! Look Pell up and down to take in her vigor and the way she looks in what she's wearing.
turns.summary: Corvin left to dress the sow; Silas and Pell take the north corridor after the bull, Huginn leading.
turns.narration:
The sow is already Corvin's work. You step back from it, wipe the spear-butt on the sedge grass, and say it easily: "Corvin — she's yours. Aldric's account, one sow, clean kill. We'll find the bull."

Corvin doesn't look up from the dressing. A short grunt — acknowledgment, not argument. He's already got the belly open. The smokehouse gets its meat; the arrangement holds.

You turn to Pell. She's still standing east of the tree-line, arms loose, weight settled the way she stands at the anvil — like she has more time than the situation requires. The light is still grey and thin, the kind that comes before the sun clears the ridge, and it sits on her evenly: the dark apron gone for the morning, a heavy linen over a lighter one, work trousers tucked into boot-tops, her hair held back with a twist of cord that has started to come undone on one side. She is built for sustained effort, not display, and she is paying no attention whatsoever to how she looks — which is, in its own way, the whole thing.

"More hunt ahead," you say. The tone is something between a statement of fact and an invitation. You nod northward — toward the corridor where the bull's track went, where Huginn's beak is still pointing. She reads it in a breath: you're asking her north with you. Her expression doesn't shift much, but her weight moves forward a fraction off the tree-line, which is answer enough.

Huginn drops off the fence-post without sound and angles north through the birches.

turns.player: Ask Huginn directly — hold your arm out and let it land, see if it will tell you what it has been threading toward.
turns.summary: Huginn lands on Silas's arm and gives one low croak — direction north, no further answer.
turns.narration:
You hold your arm out, level with your shoulder, and wait.

Huginn comes down out of the birches in three wingbeats and lands hard on your forearm — heavier than it looks, always heavier than it looks, the grip of its feet deliberate and grounding. It settles. Turns its head and looks at you with the eye that is too calm. The wood is quiet behind it, just the wet sedge dripping and Corvin's knife working steadily back at the kill.

You ask it plainly what it's been threading toward.

The bird tilts its head south — no, northwest. Holds the angle for a breath. Then it opens its beak and gives you one low croak that sits in the chest rather than the ear, the kind of sound a stone makes when it shifts in cold water. Pell goes still beside you.

That is all. It doesn't deny anything, doesn't elaborate. It hops once on your arm, resets its grip, and looks north again. The direction is clear enough. What is up there — whether it is one thing or many, whether it is dying or already dead, whether it is the bull at all — it does not say. The croak felt like *soon*, or maybe *already*, and the difference between those two things is the question it has left entirely in your hands.

turns.player: Ask Pell if she's enjoying our date so far, and tell her she looks amazing with the dew soaking her boots in the early morning sun.
turns.summary: Silas compliments Pell in the birch corridor; she receives it straight, shoulder-to-shoulder, and the hunt continues north.
turns.narration:
You fall into step beside Pell as the birch corridor opens ahead — Huginn still on your arm, its grip easing now that the question has been asked and the only answer given. The light is shifting, slowly, grey thinning toward something warmer as the ridge to the east catches the first real edge of sunrise. The dew is everywhere: on the sedge, on the bark, pooling in the boot-print the bull left in the soft ground ahead. Including, visibly, on Pell's boots — dark leather gone dark-black at the toe, a bright bead of it caught in the lacing.

You glance at her sideways and say it easy, unhurried: "You enjoying our date so far?"

She takes a beat. Then the corner of her mouth moves — not quite a smile, something drier, more contained. She looks down at her boots, then back up at the corridor ahead. "Covered in mud, up before light, listening to a bird tell us where to walk." A pause. "Yeah. I am."

"You look amazing," you tell her — and you mean it without making a production of it. The dew in the lacing, the set of her jaw in the thin morning light, the way she carries herself like someone who knows what her hands are for. "Dew and all."

She breathes out through her nose — something between a laugh and a scoff, well short of embarrassed. Her eyes don't leave the bull's track. But her shoulder brushes yours as the corridor narrows, and she doesn't step away. The contact is brief and deliberate and unbothered, which is precisely the language she uses for things she means.

Huginn shifts on your arm, head still turned north. The track is fresh. The bull is not far.

turns.player: Follow the bull's track north, reading the pattern of its movement with Order-Sense — find where it's going, not just where it's been.
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the bull's track — heading confirmed northwest toward the scrub oak ridge, a quarter-mile north.
turns.narration:
You shift your weight forward and let Order-Sense settle in — not a push, just an opening, the way you'd let your eyes adjust to dark. The bull's track is already there in the mud: three hooves clear, one dragging slightly on the left rear, the stride length telling you unhurried, heavy, a body that knows where it is going.

The pattern comes through clean. Overnight the bull moved northwest in a long unhurried arc, skirting the wet sedge where the river smell rises, favoring the drier scrub where the ground hardens between birch roots. Not fleeing — relocating. The track bends once, a deliberate angle correction, and you read it as a specific destination: higher ground, drier, with a good sightline back south. There's a low ridge a quarter-mile north where the birch thins and the scrub oak starts. He's almost certainly bedded there, or feeding along its southern edge in the early light.

Pell catches your stillness and slows beside you without asking. When you straighten and name the ridge, she looks north, reads the treeline, and nods once — the same nod she gives the anvil when she's decided where the next strike lands. Huginn shifts on your forearm, head angling northwest, the bead of its eye catching the first real edge of sunrise over the ridge. It says nothing. It doesn't need to.

turns.player: Ask Pell what she would recommend our approach be.  Also offer to let her use the spear - I can act as bait and shadowstep away at the last moment.
turns.summary: Pell takes the spear and sets the approach — Silas as bait from the east, she sets below the first oak, shadowstep is the exit plan.
turns.narration:
You slow as the birch corridor opens toward the scrub oak ridge — the trees thinning, the ground hardening under your boots, and somewhere ahead in that tangle of low oak and dry brush, a bull boar bedded or browsing in the early light. You turn to Pell.

"What do you recommend?" you say, and you lift the Deathbound Runic Spear toward her — a plain offer. "You can take it. I can draw him out. If he commits to me, I step clear at the last moment." You don't explain how. She knows enough to know you have your ways.

Pell takes the spear without ceremony — both hands, weight tested, grip found. She turns it once, reads the balance, reads the bind at the throat. Her thumb finds the socket lashing and presses it once, checking the seat. Satisfied. She holds it loose at her side and looks at the ridge for a long moment, reading the oak scrub the way she reads iron — finding what's load-bearing.

"Wind's out of the northwest," she says, flat and practical. "He's upslope. We go in from the east, stay low under the ridge-break. You go wide left — give him a clear line on you, open ground, nothing at your back. He charges downslope. That's fast." She looks at you directly. "You'll need to be fast too." Not a warning — the exact information you need, and she is trusting you to already have the answer to it. "I'll be right of center, below the first oak. When he commits, I set the butt. You get clear." One shoulder lifts. "If your way of getting clear is what it sounded like last night by the fire, now would be the time to use it."

turns.player: Does my strangeness turn you off, or on?  You know that once a woman uses a man's spear in this way, they're basically married right? (wink)
turns.summary: Silas makes a spear-marriage joke in the birch corridor; Pell absorbs it, returns a dry counterpunch, and takes her position.
turns.narration:
You let the birch corridor do half the work — the two of you close together where the trees narrow, the ridge thirty yards ahead, Huginn gone quiet on your arm. The moment has a particular texture: dew-soaked boots, grey light going gold, a large animal somewhere in the scrub ahead, and Pell with your spear in both hands looking like she knows exactly how to use it.

You say it with a straight face, mostly: "Does my strangeness turn you off, or on?" A beat. Then you let the wink land. "You know — once a woman uses a man's spear in this way, they're basically married."

Pell is quiet for exactly two seconds. Then she blows a short breath through her nose — not quite a laugh, but the shape of one, the kind that costs something to suppress. She doesn't look at you. She is watching the ridge, but her shoulders are up a fraction from where they were.

"Strangeness turns me on," she says, flat as ironwork, eyes still forward. A pause. "The married part I'll need in writing." She shifts her grip on the shaft — a small adjustment, entirely practical — and moves into her set position below the first scrub oak without another word, the ghost of something at the edge of her mouth that she doesn't bother hiding.

turns.player: GAMBIT — Hunt the bull boar together with Pell on their date
turns.summary: Gambit completed rough — bull down on the scrub oak ridge, Pell's strike clean, rune-channel backlash hit Silas hard at the kill moment.
turns.narration:
You ease east along the ridge's edge, reading shadows — birch root-wells, the undercut bank of a dried streambed, a dark notch where two scrub oaks lean together. Three good anchor points. You fix them in your mind and move to position, Huginn lifting silently from your arm to a branch above the first anchor shadow. Pell slips below the nearest oak without a sound, spear low, watching the brush. Everything in order.

Then you try to provoke the bull.

Your plan was clean: find the pain-line in the sounder's old territory, push it, give him a reason to charge east where the ground is open and the spear is waiting. What you get instead is a boar that has been on this ridge since before dawn and is in no mood to be managed. He does not charge. He surges — no warning, no grunt, no territorial display — just two hundred pounds of iron shoulder and bad intent launching from behind a stand of gorse you hadn't clocked, coming in at a flat angle from the north rather than east, tusks low and cutting. Not at you. Sideways, pushing, trying to get uphill between you and Pell's position. He is trying to split the line.

You shed your coat mid-stride — no thought, the motion just happens, the canvas thrown wide to your left where it catches his eye-line and flares on a branch. He breaks toward it for one stride. One is enough. You roll right over the dry streambed bank and come up in the shadow of the undercut — the step is instant, clean, you are simply somewhere else — and you surface five yards north with the streambed between you and where the bull was. He hits the coat, takes it on one tusk, shakes it free, and swings back to find you gone. Three seconds of pure confusion. Pell does not waste it.

She comes off the oak line at a dead run, ground-low, spear level. The bull hears her, starts his turn — and the binding in the blade sings through your thumb-bone even from here, the death-register reading the proximity of the kill. She drives the spear home behind the shoulder, full weight behind the socket, and the shaft holds — holds — and then something goes wrong in the rune-work. The channel you threaded into the blade last night at the forge-pit seizes, not releases. The Palework you were meant to push through it at the moment of the strike finds no path — the runes close like a fist around the energy instead of passing it, and what comes back up the thread is a hard concussive jolt, a sound like a bell struck inside your chest, and then your knees are in the dirt at the streambed's edge and your vision is white at the corners.

The bull is down. Pell's strike was clean enough without the craft behind it — the spear's iron did its work even if the binding failed its half of the bargain.
