You agree, and while the forge settles into the clean rhythm of Pell's work — scriber, caliper, the first ring of hammer on iron — you pull one of Aldric's trail parcels from your pack and sit on the low stool near the door.

The parcel is cloth-wrapped and knotted the way Aldric does everything: practically, without waste. You work the knot loose and peel it back. Dried river perch and salted pork, dense and a little sharp with the cold. You eat slowly, watching the morning come through the open door — pale gold on the south lane stones, a dog moving somewhere two properties over, the bell tower still quiet. Huginn drops from the rafter to the anvil horn and stands over Memory for a moment, then moves to the bench edge nearest you and watches you chew with the expression it always has, which is patience.

Pell doesn't look up from the sleeve work, but she speaks into the iron. 'You should have eaten hours ago.' It isn't a reprimand. It's the same register she uses when she notices something that ought to be corrected — factual, without heat. She drives a half-punch into the blank's edge to seat the cut line and sets it over the south pit to warm. 'Bell rings the second quarter, we go.' The sleeve isn't done yet, but the shape of the next half-hour is fixed and she's already moving inside it.

The parcel is about half gone when you feel the edge come back — not much, just the difference between a body running on nothing and a body running on something. Outside, the lane is brightening. Memory sits on the anvil horn, capacitor flush at the collar, and Huginn has not moved from the bench edge, and the east channel is twenty minutes north of the upper meadow, and the bell hasn't rung yet.

turns.player: Stay quiet and watch Pell work the sleeve — you've been up all night with her and there's something in just letting the craft be the craft for half an hour.
turns.summary: Pell finishes the sleeve job in quiet craft. She places Memory in Silas's hands and they prepare to walk north.
turns.narration:
The sleeve blank takes shape under Pell's hands in the unhurried way that competence makes look like ease. She works the punch with the heel of her palm, not the hammer — two measured taps, a quarter-turn, two more — and the iron opens along the line she scribed without complaint. Huginn watches from the anvil horn, one foot tucked, head angled. Memory catches the fire's light along the capacitor collar.

You sit on the low stool and let it be. The south pit fire breathes. A rooster sounds off somewhere past the well and is not answered. Pell holds the blank up, checks the mouth against the caliper face, sets it back to the fire. She does not look over at you. She does not need to. The shared quiet after a full night of close work has a different texture than ordinary silence — not empty, just used.

The bell tower rings the second quarter while the sleeve is still on the fire. Pell counts two beats after the last note, pulls it, and sets it to cool on the stone ledge. The whole job has taken twenty-six minutes. She peels her gloves off, tucks them under her arm, and moves the blank to the door-step for the farmer to collect.

'Ready when you are,' she says, not turning around — already moving toward the peg where her coat hangs. The river whetstone is still on the shelf above the bellows. Memory is still on the anvil horn. She lifts it, weighs it once in her palm, and sets it into your hands without ceremony. 'Bring this. If there's iron to read, you'll want it close.'

turns.player: Stop at Mara's store on the way north — the ledger may name the date the first iron valve failed, and a five-minute detour is worth a confirmed timeline.
turns.summary: Mara's ledger confirms the timeline: four sleeve failures in three weeks, starting World-day minus-eighteen at Fen's intake.
turns.narration:
The south lane is quiet at this hour — dew still on the stones, the cookhouse smoke just starting to thicken. You and Pell walk in step, Memory easy in your grip, Huginn riding your left shoulder with the companionable weight of something that has nowhere else to be. Mara's store is three minutes from the forge, red door shut but not latched; the lamp inside is already lit.

Mara looks up from her ledger when you push the door open — registers the two of you, the spear, the hour — and nods once. No preamble. 'Early start,' she says, which is not a question.

Pell stays in the doorway while you ask about the east-channel valve dates. Mara turns two pages without hesitation, runs a finger down a column of small, dense script, and stops. 'Fen's intake.' She turns the ledger to face you without lifting it from the counter. The entry is brief: a materials note for a replacement sleeve blank, a date, and Fen's name initialed in the margin. World-day minus-eighteen — roughly three weeks before today. 'I sold Fen the blank. She came back four days later to say it hadn't seated and she'd had to cap the line.' Mara closes the ledger. 'The second?'

'The farmer's — east furlong,' you say.

Mara opens a different log. A beat. 'World-day minus-nine. Also a sleeve blank. Also came back.' She looks up. 'I've sold four sleeve blanks in three weeks. Normal is two a season.' She says it the way she says everything — flat, precise, without alarm — but she holds your gaze a half-second longer than inventory warrants. 'You're going to see someone about this.'

'We are,' Pell says from the doorway.

Mara nods and lifts the ledger closed. She tears a small page from a loose notepad on the counter — two dates, two names, four blanks total — and sets it at the edge for you to take. No charge named. No explanation offered.

turns.player: Ask Mara if she knows who Dara Holt is — the senior east-channel irrigator — and whether Mara thinks she should know about this before or after you've seen the site.
turns.summary: Mara named Dara Holt — senior east-channel irrigator, house with blue gate-post on the way north — and advised seeing the site before briefing her.
turns.narration:
The question lands without preamble. Mara's chin lifts a fraction — not surprise, recognition.

"Dara Holt." She says the name the way you'd set down a tool you know well. "Senior irrigator, east channel, twenty-two years. She lives at the bend before the upper meadow — the house with the blue gate-post, you'll pass it on the way." She pauses, glancing at the note page she tore for you, then at Pell in the doorway. "After. She'll want to see it herself before she hears about it. You tell her first and she'll be halfway there before you've finished the sentence — and she'll miss something because she's hurrying."

Pell makes a small sound from the doorway — not quite agreement, but close.

Mara tucks the ledger under the counter with a flat pat. "Dara's practical. She won't panic, and she won't let politics run ahead of the evidence. But she has to be the one standing at the site." A beat. "She and Fen share a water-right on that east bend. If Fen's intake failed first, she may already have her suspicions. Don't be surprised if she's been there recently."

You pick up the valve failure timeline note from the counter edge. Four dates, two names, Mara's clean hand. The lamp flickers once as a door somewhere on the south lane opens and closes.

Outside, the morning is coming on. The blue gate-post Mara described is somewhere between here and the site.

dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.name: Dara Holt, the Ditch-Mother
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.role: Senior irrigator responsible for the valley's eastern channel network
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.voiceHints: Broad-shouldered, mid-fifties, hands that look like they were carved from the same hardwood as a shovel handle. Speaks in measurements and seasons — 'that valve's been weeping since last thaw,' not 'something's wrong.' Never dramatizes. Brings a cracked fitting to prove the point before she finishes the sentence. Calls a problem by its part name, not by its consequence.
knowledge: Forty years of the eastern channels — every slope, silting pattern, and freeze-line in the network

The river's mineral load has shifted since late autumn: fittings that lasted a decade are threading out or snapping in under two seasons, and the corrosion pattern runs inward from the joint, not outward the way sediment wear does
Three other irrigators on the eastern bench are seeing the same losses but haven't connected them yet — she has
The stretch of river above Holt's intake smells faintly wrong in still mornings, a faint bitterness she can't name but recognizes as new
She keeps a tin of failed fittings sorted by failure date and location — a physical record no one asked her to keep
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.wants: Replacement valve stock she can trust, a forge-backed answer for why her iron is failing, and confirmation that the river is the cause — not her installation work. She is not here to be reassured; she is here for a diagnosis.
dara-holt-the-ditch-mother.fears: That the river is doing something the valley has never budgeted for and that this spring's planting window will close before anyone in authority admits it.

kit.reveal: Brings physical evidence — dated, sorted, labeled — that makes the river's pattern undeniable to anyone willing to look at the parts
kit.bind: Connects isolated reports from neighboring irrigators into a coherent problem, naming the network's shared vulnerability before it fragments into blame

reactsToReputation.competent-and-direct: opens her tin of failed fittings immediately, shares dates and locations without being asked
reactsToReputation.dismissive-or-theoretical: polite, brief, and gone — she'll find someone who works with their hands
reactsToReputation.curious-and-honest: stays longer than she planned, starts drawing channel maps on whatever surface is available

establishedFacts.text: Silas arrived in Millbrook on World-day 12 as a complete stranger — no reputation, no contacts, pack still on his back.
Silas Weir raised a cleaned, dead rabbit back to life in Millbrook's upper meadow on World-day 12 — no ritual, no understood mechanism; the valley's old grain answered something in him. The animal is

Silas struck a provisioning deal with Aldric the smokehouse man on World-day 12 — hunting in exchange for food and supplies, with Aldric asking for any information Silas finds about the upstream conta
Silas made his first hunting delivery to Aldric on World-day 12 — two rabbits and the pigeon. Aldric provided preserved trail food in return. The provisioning arrangement is now active.
Silas struck a labor deal with Pell the blacksmith — clearing her edge-work backlog in exchange for hunting company north of the village. Work begins now.
Pell agreed to join a three-way boar hunt in the north wood — modification on the light spear-tip at cost (covered by Silas), Corvin to be invited. Hunt departs once the forge backlog is clear.
Pell's forge backlog fully cleared on World-day 12 morning — all five pieces done including the hand-axe heat-collar. Pell confirmed ready to walk north once Corvin is found.
Silas gave Pell the fine river whetstone as a gift for their planned date — she received it with genuine quiet pleasure and set it on her bench.
Corvin committed to the hunt — north gate, tomorrow morning before the light gets high. Hunt party fully assembled: Silas Weir, Pell Marsh, Corvin.
Pell agreed to meet Silas at the forge after the hunt — the date is now a confirmed arrangement between them, named plainly on the field path on World-day 13.

The assembled mid-weight spear now carries a rough death-binding — runes load-bearing in shaft and blade, Pell Marsh's maker's mark seated in the iron, a nascent thread to Huginn. The death-register i

Pell Marsh has been told plainly that her maker's mark is load-bearing in the spear's death-binding — she has accepted this as possibly true and asked what 'grows into' means practically. She now know

Pell Marsh has agreed to meet Silas at the forge after the hunt to be shown what he was doing with grain-reading and to investigate the gift the iron showed in her — a provisional, interest-driven agr
Sow taken cleanly in the north wood on World-day 14 pre-dawn — Corvin's strike, Silas's hold. Piglets scattered north. Bull's track and Huginn's northward orientation remain unresolved.

Bull boar taken on the scrub oak ridge on World-day 14 morning — Pell's strike, spear held. Silas's Palework channel through the runes misfired at the kill moment: the binding closed rather than passe
Silas paid Corvin twenty cord-weight from the hunt proceeds — unprompted, before Corvin could leave. Corvin received it without ceremony and departed for his furlong.
Silas paid Pell forty cord-weight, framed as forge-afternoon materials. She accepted it, said she'd have the fire up, and left the smokehouse.
Pell has formally agreed to plan the Deathbound Runic Spear repair with Silas — the repair is now a shared undertaking between them, with Huginn attending.
The Deathbound Runic Spear has been upgraded — transverse channel deepened, maker's mark re-seated (slightly oblique), memory-capacitor installed rough at the collar north quarter. Death-register now
The Deathbound Runic Spear is fully repaired and seated — all known faults addressed, memory-capacitor flush at the collar. No remaining structural issues as of World-day 14 dawn.
The Deathbound Runic Spear has been named Memory — the name given by Silas at dawn in Pell's forge, in the presence of Pell and Huginn, over wine and old starfruit.
Pell told Silas plainly at dawn that she does not know what he is or where he's going, but she'd like to keep finding out — an explicit, named interest on her part.
Silas told Pell plainly he'd fallen for her — she held his wrist and said 'not yet', neither pulling away nor deflecting. The relationship has moved past mutual interest into something with weight.

Silas kissed Pell at the forge bench at dawn on World-day 14 — she held his wrist, stayed, and said 'alright'. The relationship has moved past named mutual interest into something with physical weight
Pell agreed to walk north with Silas after the farmer's sleeve job to inspect the failed east-channel valve sites — Fen's intake is the first destination, twenty minutes north of the upper meadow.

the-attended-end.name: The Attended End
the-attended-end.description: Marrow tells you plainly what is ending here and how long it has — a person, a truce, a building, a road. It is never wrong and it never softens it.
the-attended-end.notFor: Preventing the ending; being asked to be gentle about it; anything that is not, in fact, ending.
the-attended-end.narrationHints: Marrow tells you plainly what is ending here and how long it has — a person, a truce, a building, a road. It is never wrong and it never softens it.

corrections.why: SNG-068/085 — creation committed before confirm: the prologue derived his domains and abilities from paths he played, not from the character he chose. He is a NECROMANCER; the game made him a wright-domain character with Blazeborn/Lattice/Numinous abilities, and his breadth cap (3 at level 2) was fully spent on them, locking him out of his own people's crafts entirely.

changes.note: the death pole. ORIGIN stays 'wright' — he is a Wright who FINISHED something and was nearly exiled for it, and now practices the craft of ending. That contradiction is the best thing about him and it is not a bug.

NET ZERO — 3 before, 3 after. A repair, not an advance. order_sense KEPT at Erik's request; it is 4 steps from his secondary (penalized, legal) and it buys THE MEASURED RUIN (wither + order_sense): 'decay applied with a designer's precision — this beam and not that one'. That is a smart necromancer.

corrections.notGranted: No xp, no levels, no skill points, no items. His 1 skill point is untouched — he spends it himself.
